title: Presentation of Jesus in the Temple
context:
# TEMPORAL CONTEXT SNAPSHOT
## Saint John's Episcopal Cathedral, Denver, Colorado
## February 2, 2020 | "Presentation of Jesus in the Temple"

---

LITURGICAL CONTEXT

February 2 is the Feast of the Presentation of Jesus in the Temple (Candlemas), a fixed observance in the church calendar falling 40 days after Christmas. In 2020, this feast fell on a Sunday, making it the primary liturgical observance. The RCL readings would have included Malachi 3:1-4, Psalm 84, Hebrews 2:14-18, and Luke 2:22-40, focusing on Jesus's presentation and Simeon's prophecy. The church was in the season after Epiphany, transitioning toward Lent (which would begin February 26 that year), still in the post-Christmas theological arc.

EPISCOPAL / ANGLICAN CHURCH NEWS

The Episcopal Church was in a relatively stable denominational period, though ongoing tensions surrounded LGBTQ+ inclusion and biblical interpretation continued from the 2018 General Convention. Bishop Robert Wright of the Diocese of Colorado had been serving since 2013 and would have been actively engaged with parishes like Saint John's. No major denominational crises or bishop elections were imminent in early February 2020. The church's attention was beginning to turn toward the pandemic's early stages, though it had not yet become the defining crisis of 2020.

DENVER & COLORADO NEWS

Denver had experienced mild winter weather through late January 2020, with temperatures above normal for early February. The Colorado legislature was in session (began January 8), debating education funding and healthcare issues. Downtown Denver and the Capitol Hill area where the cathedral is located were relatively quiet, with no major local crises dominating headlines in the week prior. The city's attention was focused on local governance and the approaching 2020 election cycle.

NATIONAL NEWS (US)

The nation was intensely focused on the impeachment trial of President Donald Trump in the Senate, which concluded with acquittal on February 5, 2020 (three days after this sermon). The Iowa caucuses had just occurred on February 3 (the day after this sermon), marking the official start of the 2020 primary campaign. The first confirmed COVID-19 death in the United States occurred on February 6 in California, though the virus was not yet a major national news focus. The Super Bowl had aired just four days earlier (February 2 or Super Bowl Sunday), with the Kansas City Chiefs defeating the San Francisco 49ers.

INTERNATIONAL NEWS

The COVID-19 pandemic was accelerating in China and beginning to concern global health officials, though international travel restrictions were not yet in place. The World Health Organization had declared COVID-19 a "Public Health Emergency of International Concern" on January 30, 2020, but was not yet using the term "pandemic." Iran, Italy, and South Korea would not see significant outbreaks for another week or two, so the global severity was not yet apparent to most Western observers.

CULTURAL MOMENT

American culture was in a moment of political polarization and uncertainty, with the impeachment dominating national discourse and primary season beginning. The nascent awareness of COVID-19 existed primarily among public health officials and international news watchers; most Americans were not yet concerned about a pandemic. Denver's cultural mood was typical for early winter—post-holiday, focused on spring planning and the coming election. The congregation at Saint John's would have gathered in a moment of relative normalcy, unaware that within weeks the pandemic would transform worship, community gathering, and American life fundamentally.
